description: Plain white rice paired with properly cooked Nigerian honey beans — rich with palm oil, crayfish, and deep seasoning. Two separate components, one perfect plate.

ingredients.name: parboiled long grain rice
ingredients.note: Wash until water runs completely clear before cooking.

ingredients.name: honey beans (oloyin)
ingredients.note: Honey beans are sweeter and creamier. Brown beans or black-eyed peas work but change the flavour significantly.

ingredients.name: palm oil
ingredients.note: This is non-negotiable for Nigerian beans. Vegetable oil is a poor substitute — flavour will be flat.

ingredients.name: onion
ingredients.note: 1 for the beans, 1 sliced to fry in the beans base.

ingredients.name: scotch bonnet pepper (ata rodo)
ingredients.note: Blend with the onion. Skip for no heat, or use 1 small for a background warmth.

ingredients.name: ground crayfish
ingredients.note: The defining flavour of Nigerian beans. Do not skip — this is what makes it taste right.

ingredients.name: seasoning cubes
ingredients.note: Maggi or Knorr. Crush before adding.

ingredients.name: salt
ingredients.note: Season beans at the end — salting too early makes beans tough and slows cooking.

ingredients.name: smoked fish (optional)
ingredients.note: Adds incredible depth. Debone and crumble into the beans. Dried catfish or smoked mackerel both work.

ingredients.name: ponmo (cow skin)
ingredients.note: Traditional addition for texture. Pre-cook until soft before adding to beans.

ingredients.name: water
ingredients.note: For both the beans and the rice. Keep hot water nearby for topping up beans.

steps.title: Sort and Wash the Beans
steps.instruction: Pick through the dry beans and remove any stones, shrivelled beans, or debris. Rinse thoroughly under cold water 2–3 times. Soak in cold water for at least 30 minutes if time allows — this reduces cooking time significantly. Drain before cooking.
steps.sensory_cue: Beans should look plump and even-coloured after soaking. Water will darken — that is normal, discard it.
steps.failure_risk: Skipping the sort step risks biting into a stone. Never season beans before they are soft — salt at this stage causes beans to stay hard no matter how long you cook them.

steps.title: First Boil — Parboil and Drain
steps.instruction: Place washed beans in a pot. Cover with plenty of cold water (about 3x the volume of beans). Bring to a hard boil over high heat and cook for 15 minutes. Drain and discard this water completely. This removes the gas-causing compounds and the raw bean taste. Return beans to the pot.
steps.sensory_cue: The water will turn dark and foamy — this is exactly what you want to throw away. The beans will look slightly swollen but still firm.
steps.failure_risk: Skipping this step causes bloating and leaves an unpleasant raw bean taste. It also significantly extends final cooking time.

steps.title: Cook Beans Until Tender
steps.instruction: Cover parboiled beans with fresh hot water (enough to sit 5cm above the beans). Add 1 roughly chopped onion. Cook over medium-high heat, covered, until beans are completely soft — they should crush easily between two fingers with no resistance. Top up with hot water whenever the level drops below the beans. Do not add salt yet.
steps.sensory_cue: A properly cooked bean crushes between your fingers without effort and tastes creamy, not chalky. The cooking liquid should be thick and slightly starchy.
steps.failure_risk: Adding salt before beans are fully soft is the number one beans mistake in Nigerian cooking — they will never soften no matter how long you cook them. Test before seasoning.

steps.title: Make the Beans Base
steps.instruction: In a separate small pan or directly in the beans pot (if there is enough space), heat the palm oil over medium heat. Add the second sliced onion and fry until soft and fragrant, about 3 minutes. Add blended scotch bonnet if using. Fry for 2 more minutes. Add ground crayfish and stir. Pour this entire mixture into the cooked beans. Add smoked fish and ponmo if using.
steps.sensory_cue: The palm oil should melt clear-orange and the onions should sizzle immediately when they go in. The crayfish hitting the hot oil will release a distinct, savoury umami smell.
steps.failure_risk: Burning the palm oil gives it a harsh, acrid taste that ruins the beans. Medium heat only — you are sweating the onions, not deep frying.

steps.title: Season and Finish the Beans
steps.instruction: Add crushed seasoning cubes and salt to the beans. Stir well to combine everything. Taste and adjust seasoning. Cook uncovered on low heat for 10–15 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the flavours meld and the liquid reduces to a thick, saucy consistency that coats the beans. The beans should look glossy from the palm oil.
steps.sensory_cue: The finished beans should be deeply orange-red from the palm oil, smell savoury and slightly smoky from the crayfish, and have a thick sauce — not watery, not dry.
steps.failure_risk: Too much water left in the beans makes it soupy. Cook it down. Too dry and it will stick — keep stirring and add a splash of hot water if needed.

steps.title: Cook the White Rice
steps.instruction: While beans is finishing, bring a pot of salted water to a rolling boil (use about 2x the volume of rice in water). Add the washed rice. Stir once. Cook uncovered over medium-high heat until rice is just tender — not mushy, not crunchy. Drain in a sieve immediately. Return to the pot, cover, and let steam off heat for 5 minutes.
steps.sensory_cue: White rice is done when a grain pressed between your fingers is fully soft with no hard centre. The water will look starchy and slightly cloudy when it is ready to drain.
steps.failure_risk: Overcooking white rice past the drain point turns it mushy. Drain as soon as it is just done — residual heat finishes it in the pot.

steps.title: Rest and Serve
steps.instruction: Fluff the rice gently with a fork. Taste the beans one final time — adjust salt if needed. Serve rice and beans side by side on the same plate. The beans acts as both a sauce and a protein component.
steps.sensory_cue: The plate should have the contrast of white fluffy rice against deep orange-red glossy beans. The smell rising should be warm, savoury, and distinctly Nigerian.
